question_data.text: The term Hire Purchase refers to a rental agreement with an option to buy goods payable by:
question_data.explanation: Hire purchase is characterized by periodic payments over time.

question_data.text: In a Hire Purchase agreement, the person who transfers possession of the goods is called the:
question_data.explanation: The hire vendor is the owner who lets out the goods.

question_data.text: Under a Hire Purchase agreement, the ownership of the asset remains with the seller or financing company until:
question_data.explanation: Ownership only transfers once all instalments and the option to purchase fee are paid.

question_data.text: The first judicial recognition of a contract of Hire Purchase was in the House of Lords case of:
options: Incar Motors Nigeria Ltd vs. Elias Bus Transport Ltd
Amusa & Anor vs. Bentworth Finance Nig Ltd
question_data.explanation: Helby v Mathews (1895) is the landmark case establishing the nature of hire purchase.

question_data.text: The Nigerian law that regulates the Hire Purchase transaction is primarily the:
question_data.explanation: The 1965 Act is the primary statutory framework for hire purchase in Nigeria.

question_data.text: Which of the following is NOT a basic feature of a Hire Purchase agreement?
options: It is a form of bailment
The owner retains property rights until full payment
It confers an obligation to purchase
It confers an option to purchase
question_data.explanation: Hire purchase confers an option, not an obligation; the latter would be a conditional sale.

question_data.text: If the owner reclaims goods and sells them for an amount more than the debt owed by the hirer, the owner is:
options: Bound to return the balance to the hirer
Entitled to recover the balance from the hirer
Not bound to return the balance to the hirer
Liable to the hirer for damages
question_data.explanation: In common law hire purchase, the owner is entitled to the full value of the repossessed goods.

question_data.text: Under the Hire Purchase Act of 1965, the agreement must be in which form?
question_data.explanation: The Act requires a written memorandum signed by the parties.

question_data.text: For goods other than motor vehicles, the Hire Purchase Act of 1965 generally applies only if the price of the goods in question does not exceed:
question_data.explanation: The Act's protection is capped at N2,000 for non-motor vehicle items.

question_data.text: One of the main disadvantages of Hire Purchase agreements is that they tend to be more expensive in the long run due to:
question_data.explanation: The cost of credit (interest) makes the total hire purchase price higher than the cash price.

question_data.text: The Hire Purchase Act of 1965 applied only to the Federal Capital Territory of Lagos at the time of enactment, but was extended to all parts of the federation by the:
question_data.explanation: The 1966 Application Act made the 1965 Act nationwide.

question_data.text: Under the Common Law, a hirer's default on payment, even for the last installment, meant the owner's right to recover the goods was:
question_data.explanation: Common law was strict and allowed repossession upon any default.

question_data.text: A contract of Hire Purchase is distinguished from a Credit Sale primarily because in a Credit Sale:
options: Ownership transfers immediately to the buyer
The seller retains the risk and liability
The buyer has an option to return the goods
question_data.explanation: In credit sales, ownership passes at the time of agreement.

question_data.text: A key distinction between Hire Purchase and a Conditional Credit Sale is that the latter confers on the buyer:
question_data.explanation: A conditional sale obligates the buyer to finish payments and take title.

question_data.text: Unlike Hire Purchase, a simple Contract of Hire means the hirer has:
options: An option to buy the goods
No intention of eventually owning the asset
question_data.explanation: Standard hire (renting) does not involve a path to ownership.

question_data.text: Failure to comply with the formalities prescribed in S. 2 of the Hire Purchase Act generally disentitles the owner from:
options: Recovering the full price of the goods
Enforcing the agreement or recovering the goods
question_data.explanation: Strict compliance is required for the owner to enforce their rights under the Act.

question_data.text: The provision of S. 3(a) of the Hire Purchase Act prevents the owner from inserting any term that would give him the right to:
options: Enter the hirer's premises without authorisation to seize the goods
question_data.explanation: Protecting the hirer's privacy/security against forceful repossession.

question_data.text: One of the implied conditions in a Hire Purchase contract, as provided by S. 4 of the Act, is that the goods must be:
options: Fit for the purpose for which they were required
Returned if the contract is terminated
question_data.explanation: Fitness for purpose is a standard implied condition.

question_data.text: According to the Act, the implied condition that the goods are of merchantable quality would NOT apply if:
options: The price of the goods exceeds two thousand naira
The goods are hired for commercial purposes
The goods are hired on a second hand basis and this is stated in the memorandum
question_data.explanation: Second-hand goods carry a lower standard if disclosed.

question_data.text: The maximum liability that can be incurred by the hirer under the Minimum Payment Clause as stipulated by S. 8(1) of the Act is:
options: Three fifths (3/5) of the total price
One half (1/2) of the total Hire Purchase price
Any amount agreed upon by the parties
question_data.explanation: The Act limits the 'early termination penalty' to 50% of the price.

question_data.text: In the case of Karsales Harrow Ltd vs. Wallis, the court held that in a Hire Purchase agreement, the owner has a duty to deliver the goods:
options: In the condition they were in when they were manufactured
In the condition they were when the hirer inspected them
Before the final payment is made
question_data.explanation: Goods must match the state they were in during the pre-contract inspection.

question_data.text: According to S.9(1) of the Hire Purchase Act, after the payment of the relevant proportion, the owner cannot recover the goods except by:
options: A private agreement with the hirer
An action in the court of law
question_data.explanation: The court's protection kicks in once a significant portion of the price is paid.

question_data.text: For motor vehicles, the 'relevant proportion' of the total Hire Purchase price, after payment of which repossession requires a court order, is:
question_data.explanation: 3/5 is the specific statutory threshold for motor vehicles in Nigeria.

question_data.text: The provision of S. 3(f) of the Act prevents the owner from inserting terms that mandate the hirer to make use of:
options: An insurer or repairer of the owner's choice
question_data.explanation: Terms forcing specific insurers/repairers on the hirer are void.

question_data.text: The duty of a hirer to pay installments, as and when due, is demonstrated in the case of:
question_data.explanation: Animashawun v CFAO emphasizes the hirer's core obligation to pay.

question_data.text: In the case of Helby vs. Mathews, the House of Lords held that title to the piano still resided in the owner (plaintiff) because:
options: The hirer pledged the piano legally.
The hirer had an obligation to buy the piano.
The instalments were not completely paid.
The agreement was a contract of sale.
question_data.explanation: Until full payment, the hirer has no power to pass good title to third parties.

question_data.text: A Hire Purchase agreement only grants the hirer the right of:
question_data.explanation: Possession is granted immediately, but ownership is deferred.

question_data.text: If a hirer defaults on payment, the owner's right to reclaim the property is unqualified under:
question_data.explanation: Common law favored the owner's property rights over the hirer's equity.

question_data.text: What differentiates a contract of Hire Purchase from a pure bailment is the fact that, at the end of the contract, the hirer has:
options: An option to buy the goods in question.
A debt obligation to the owner.
question_data.explanation: Pure bailment involves return of goods; hire purchase includes a purchase option.

question_data.text: According to S. 20(1) of the Hire Purchase Act of 1965, the term 'Hire Purchase' is defined as a bailment of goods in pursuance of an agreement under which the bailee:
options: May buy the goods or property in the goods will or may pass.
Must pay the full price upfront.
question_data.explanation: The definition focuses on the potential passage of property rights.

question_data.text: The Helby vs. Mathews decision prevented a person who exercises his right to an option to buy from passing good title to an innocent purchaser for value before:
options: Making full payment to his hire vendor.
question_data.explanation: Without title, one cannot legally transfer title (Nemo dat).

question_data.text: Which of the following is an advantage of a Hire Purchase agreement for the manufacturer/seller?
question_data.explanation: Hire purchase expands the market for expensive goods by making them affordable via credit.

question_data.text: Which of the following is an element of a traditional contract/agreement that must be present in a Hire Purchase agreement?
question_data.explanation: Like any contract, hire purchase requires consideration.

question_data.text: Under the Common Law, a provision for punctual payment meant that a one-day delay in the last installment payment was regarded as a breach, entitling the owner to retrieve the goods, as held in:
options: Amusa & Anor vs. Bentworth Finance Nig Ltd.
Bentworth Finance Ltd vs. De Bank Transport Ltd.
question_data.explanation: Bentworth v De Bank is the authority for the 'punctual payment' rule.

question_data.text: For a Hire Purchase agreement to be valid under the Nigerian Act, the owner of the goods has to state in writing, to the hirer:
options: The cash price of the goods in question.
question_data.explanation: The Act requires the hirer be informed of the cash price to evaluate the credit cost.

question_data.text: The need for a written notice of cash price is dispensed with if the hirer selected the goods by reference to a price list, catalogue or:
options: Advertisement which stated the cash price of the goods.
question_data.explanation: Advertisements with cash prices satisfy the transparency requirement.

question_data.text: The memorandum of agreement for Hire Purchase must contain all the following EXCEPT:
options: A statement of the hire purchase price and cash price.
A statement of the deposit paid.
A statement of the owner's motive for selling.
The amount of each instalment and the date of payment.
question_data.explanation: Subjective motives are not required in the statutory memorandum.

question_data.text: In Adelabari vs. Nigeria Motors Ltd, the court held that the consequence for non-compliance with S. 2 of the Act could not apply because:
options: The non-compliance had not prejudiced the hirer's right.
The agreement was concluded before the existence of the Hire Purchase Act.
The agreement concerned a motor vehicle.
question_data.explanation: Statutes are generally not retroactive.

question_data.text: The discretionary power of the court to waive non-compliance with formalities is available only if the non-compliance has not:
options: Prejudiced the right of the hirer.
question_data.explanation: The court can excuse technical errors if no harm was done to the hirer.

question_data.text: Which section of the Hire Purchase Act specifically deals with the exclusion of certain exploitative terms?
question_data.explanation: Section 3 lists terms that are void if included in the agreement.

question_data.text: S. 3(b) of the Act precludes the owner from inserting any term which would exclude the hirer from exercising his right to:
options: Claim a refund of the deposit.
Bring the agreement to an end (determine the agreement).
question_data.explanation: The hirer must always have the right to terminate early (with statutory liability).

question_data.text: S. 3(d) of the Act excludes terms which state that the agent of the owner/seller would also be the agent of the:
question_data.explanation: To prevent conflict of interest, the owner's agent can't be deemed the hirer's agent.

question_data.text: One of the implied warranties in a Hire Purchase contract, as provided by S. 4 of the Act, is that the hirer shall have and enjoy:
options: The right to sell the goods.
question_data.explanation: Quiet possession is a standard implied warranty of title.

question_data.text: The implied condition that the goods are of merchantable quality would NOT apply if the defects in the goods are of the type that the owner:
options: Could not have been reasonably aware of at the time of the agreement.
question_data.explanation: Owners aren't liable for latent defects beyond reasonable inspection.

question_data.text: In a Conditional Credit Sale, if the buyer resells the goods to a third party, the buyer transfers good title under:
question_data.explanation: The Sale of Goods Act allows buyers in possession to pass title, unlike hire purchase.

question_data.text: Which of the following is a disadvantage of a Hire Purchase agreement?
options: Access to goods without large upfront payment.
Potential repossession if regular payments are not made.
Ownership at the end of the agreement.
question_data.explanation: Repossession risk is the major drawback for the hirer.

question_data.text: The legal definition of a Bailee is a person who:
options: Receives personal property from another and has possession but not title.
Has an obligation to sell the property.
Has a lien over the property.
question_data.explanation: Bailment is based on the separation of possession and ownership.

question_data.text: If the hirer has already paid a sum which is more than one half of the Hire Purchase price and terminates the agreement, he would incur:
options: Liability up to three fifths of the total price.
question_data.explanation: If payments already reach the 1/2 mark, the termination clause is satisfied.

question_data.text: When an owner recovers goods in contravention of S. 9(1) of the Act (e.g., without a court order after the relevant proportion is paid), S. 9(2) provides that:
options: The owner shall not be liable to the hirer.
The hirer shall be entitled to recover the amounts he has paid.
The hirer shall be free from all liabilities and be entitled to recover amounts paid.
The hirer is only entitled to recover the deposit.
question_data.explanation: Illegal repossession results in a total forfeiture by the owner.

question_data.text: The duty of a hirer to use the goods in ways that would not occasion damage to the goods is called:
options: Duty not to sell the Goods.
question_data.explanation: Hirers must treat the goods as a 'prudent owner' would.
